time_posted: I set up 37 AI Agents for our $6M ARR outbound agency.
hook: These are the 6 AI Agents we deploy across our >$10M ARR clients.
full_content:
These are the 6 AI Agents we deploy across our >$10M ARR clients. 


I used to spend HOURS trying to figure out what makes a cold email work.

Which pain points hit hardest.

What signals show someone is ready to buy.

When to reach out.

How to segment my lists without losing my mind.

Now?
I run everything through a squad of AI agents (built in n8n) that do the heavy lifting for me.

Here’s the team:

→ 1. COMPANY_ANALYST
Analyzes your website, case studies, and G2 reviews.
Finds what problems you solve, what ROI you deliver, and what makes you different.

Outputs:
• Top pain points (ranked 1-10)
• Customer impact metrics
• Differentiation hooks
• Real customer language

→ 2. PAIN_EXPERT
Takes those insights and builds a Pain Point Matrix.
Scores each pain by:
• Frequency
• Financial impact
• Time savings
• Risk reduction
• Emotional relief
• Urgency
Then ranks them-so you know what matters MOST.

→ 3. SIGNAL_HUNTER
Searches for digital breadcrumbs showing a company feels that pain.
Looks at:
• Tech stack
• Website copy
• Job posts
• Social posts
• Event attendance
• Review activity
Even gives you ready-to-use Boolean search strings for LinkedIn.
(Yes, you get a literal playbook for signals.)

→ 4. SEGMENT_STRATEGIST
Breaks your market into micro-segments (100-200 companies each).
Maps the most intense pain for each.
Defines how to spot them, what triggers that pain, and what NOT to target.
Helps you focus on the best-fit group first.

→ 5. TRIGGER_SPECIALIST
Watches for buying signals in that top segment:
• Researching solutions?
• Budget approved?
• Leadership change?
• Tech stack updates?
Sets up real-time alerts and tells you exactly when/how to reach out.

→ 6. CAMPAIGN_BUILDER
Takes all this and builds 3 outbound campaigns you can launch.
For each:
• Campaign name
• Target audience
• Trigger event
• Messaging
• Data sources
• Personalization fields
• Target KPIs
• A/B test plan
• Launch checklist

If you want to see how these AI agents actually work in a real outbound workflow (step-by-step) - I'll be putting together an entire SOP over the weekend, let me know if you want it!

10 hours X 8 reps = 80 hrs. That's at least $10,000 saved monthly.

The Problem: Spending 20+ minutes researching each prospect manually.

Switching between LinkedIn, company websites, and Google. Forgetting key details mid-call.

time_posted: If I were to launch a company tomorrow, here's the GTM Cheatsheet I would follow to get to $1,000,000 ARR.
hook: EMAIL INFRASTRUCTURE
full_content:
EMAIL INFRASTRUCTURE

We use 20 secondary domains (never primary), 40 mailboxes with unique IPs, and send 20-25 emails per mailbox daily.

Every mailbox gets a 2-week warm-up before campaigns.

Technical stack:
→ SPF, DKIM, DMARC authentication
→ Domain forwarding to primary
→ Replace domains every 9-12 months

This setup lands 95%+ emails in the primary inbox.

LEAD GENERATION SIGNALS

We track 10+ buying signals before outreach.
Company signals:
→ Hiring surge (5+ new roles)
→ Fresh funding rounds
→ Office expansion
→ New tech stack
→ Leadership changes

Behavioral signals:
→ Website visits (Vector tracks this)
→ Content downloads
→ LinkedIn profile views
→ Email engagement
→ Competitor research

Goal: Catch them when they are looking.

EMAIL SEQUENCE TIMING

SMB: 5-8 touchpoints over 30 days.
Day 1: Initial email
Day 3: Follow-up
Day 5: LinkedIn connection
Day 8: Value-add email
Day 12: Case study
Day 17: Final attempt

Enterprise: 10-18 touchpoints over 60 days.
Longer gaps, multi-channel (email, LinkedIn, calls, DM)
50% emails, 50% other channels
More education, less spam

GTM WORKFLOWS

6 core automations (all n8n + Make):
→ Ghosting: Auto-close deals with no activity >30 days
→ Discovery: Trigger sequences for cold leads
→ Offer Tracking: Follow up on proposals
→ Closed Won: Onboarding tasks
→ Lead Creation: Route inbound leads
→ Sales Handoff: Notify AEs on qualified leads

EMAIL METRICS THAT MATTER

Ignore open rates and CTR. They are unreliable and can hurt deliverability.

Track reply rate:
Industry: 1-5%
Good: 10%+
Great: 25%+

Positive Reply Rate (PRR):
Industry: 0.1-0.5%
Good: 1-3%
Exceptional: 4 meetings per 100 leads


REPORTING & KPIs

Daily dashboard covers:
Deliverability rate (>95%)
Reply rate (15%+)
Positive replies (1-5%)
Meetings booked per rep
Show rate (70-80%)
SQL conversion (25-35%)
Close rate (20-35%)
Spam rate (<0.1%)
Bounce rate (<3%)
Domain reputation score

We review these weekly, and adjust.

P.S: What's the ONE metric you focus on most for cold email

time_posted: Instantly.ai analyzed 1,000,000 cold emails so you don’t have to.
hook: Here’s the 9-step system that gets 20–30% replies.
full_content:
Here’s the 9-step system that gets 20–30% replies.

1️⃣ STRONG OFFER

Your ICP is not “VP Sales, 50–500 employees”.

You need:
- The outcome they want
- The pain that keeps them awake
- The words they use to talk about it

Miss here and every email fails.

2️⃣ HYPERENRICHED DATA

Name + email is not enough.

Pull:
- Job posts
- Tech stack
- Funding
- Case studies
- LinkedIn activity

More signals = sharper angles = real relevance.

3️⃣ AI PERSONALIZED LINES

“Hope you are doing well” → delete.

“Saw you are hiring 3 AEs after your Series B” → reply.

Use AI to scale research, not to write fluff.

4️⃣ MASTER THE FUNDAMENTALS

Get obsessed with:
- Who you target (ICP)
- What outcome you give (offer)
- How you show value (copy)

Depth wins.

5️⃣ SMALLER, SMARTER LISTS

500 laser-targeted > 100,000 spray and pray.

Smaller lists:
- Sharper copy
- Cleaner data
- Better deliverability

6️⃣ FOLLOW-UP PROTOCOL

Most replies are not from Email 1.

Run a 4-touch sequence:
Email 1: Personalized opener
Email 2: Short bump (3–5 days)
Email 3: New angle (3–5 days)
Email 4: Breakup (3–5 days)

Short, direct, adds new value.

7️⃣ VALUE-DRIVEN EMAILS

Stop asking for meetings first line.

Lead with:
- “Happy to share what is working best right now”
- “Want a quick example?”
- “Can I send something that helps with [pain]?”

You earn calls by giving value.

8️⃣ LONG GAME MINDSET

Cold email is a system, not a stunt.

You build:
- Consistent pipeline
- Cleaner data
- Better offers

Quality > speed. Presence > pressure.

9️⃣ DELIVERABILITY

Top 1% know inbox health is the real boss.

You need:
- Multiple domains
- Multiple inboxes
- 30–50 sends per inbox per day
- Full technical setup
- 30-day warm-up

Anything you think is missing here?

Here’s the exact 7-stage framework I used for my client.

1️⃣ ICP Foundation

Start with a deep dive. I pull three data points:

→ Intake questionnaire
→ Reverse-engineer best deals
→ Talk with the sales team

This shows who actually converts. No guessing. Only proof.

2️⃣ Total Market Capture

I map the full market using:

→ Apollo for company data
→ LinkedIn Sales Navigator for roles
→ Ocean.io and DiscoLike for lookalike expansion

All data flows into Clay. Here, I:

→ Remove duplicates
→ Cut poor-fit accounts
→ Standardize everything
→ Apply strict qualification
→ Split into segments

Clean lists win deals.

3️⃣ Intelligence Layer

Each account gets enriched with:

→ Industry vertical
→ Headcount changes
→ Tech stack (including competitors)
→ Security certifications
→ Funding signals
→ Revenue model and business context

I add live signals:

→ Trigify.io for social activity
→ Clay for job changes
→ RB2B/Vector 👻 for website visits

I know who’s moving and who’s interested-right now.

4️⃣ Account Prioritization

Every account gets scored and tiered using:

→ Clay
→ Unify
→ Claude

Tier 1: High-touch (calls, custom research)
Tier 2: Blended (email + social)
Tier 3: Scaled automation (email at volume)

Resources go where the highest ROI lives.

5️⃣ Contact Discovery

I map the buying committee by:

→ Department
→ Influence level (user, decision-maker, champion)

Clay finds emails and phone numbers-fast, accurate, repeatable.

6️⃣ Messaging Optimization

I test everything:

→ Opening hooks
→ Value props
→ Proof points
→ Personalization depth
→ Call-to-action framing

Instantly.ai and lemlist deliver at scale.

7️⃣ Amplification

When a campaign works, I scale it up:

→ Add more rep capacity
→ Target new buyer personas
→ Pull in fresh data sources
→ Copy what’s working, fast

But the real engine is integration.

Layer outbound with:

→ Organic content
→ Paid media
→ Partnerships
→ Community

full_content:
Here's how to build your first AI automation (5 mins or less):

"AI automation is too technical"

"Workflow builders are too complicated"

"You need developers to build smart automations"

Let me debunk that:

You can build AI workflows fast if you know the right tool.

(Now you've got yourself an AI automation cheatsheet.)

My n8n workflows typically generate:

→ 80% less manual work

→ 24/7 intelligent lead qualification

→ Automatic pipeline enrichment that thinks

"But AI automation is so hard to get right."

Ignore the complexity for a sec and focus on the fundamentals.

Here's how to get started:

1️⃣ Go to (n8n.io)and create a free account

2️⃣ Click "Create Workflow"

3️⃣ Add your first node (Gmail, Google Sheets, OpenAI, or whatever)

4️⃣ Connect the blocks, drag from one to the next

5️⃣ Add AI logic: let ChatGPT/Claude make decisions in your workflow

Example workflow you can build in 5 minutes:

→ New website visitor triggers the flow

→ AI researches their company

→ Decides if they're a good fit

→ Auto-adds to CRM with smart notes

→ Sends personalized follow-up

I've seen simple 3-node workflows outperform complex enterprise systems.

Not because they had more features, but because they:

a. Solved one problem well (focus)

b. Actually worked reliably (simplicity)

c. Could run without breaking (maintainability)

The difference between n8n and Zapier? n8n lets AI make smart decisions in your workflows instead of just rigid "if this, then that" logic.

If you want to automate your outbound without hiring more people, start with one simple workflow that thinks for itself.

What's your biggest manual task right now?

time_posted: I fell down the rabbit hole studying Snowflake's outbound engine.
hook: $4,000,000,000 ARR. 60% from SDRs. Here's the system:
full_content:
$4,000,000,000 ARR. 60% from SDRs. Here's the system:


Marketing, SDRs, and Sales work as ONE team with a 5-step framework that pre-warms every account before outreach.

The result: 46% meeting rate (vs 17% for regions that ignored this).

Here's the breakdown:

1️⃣ Collaboration
No more sales vs. marketing blame game.

They work as one team:
↳Same goals
↳Same messaging
↳Same account list

Marketing provides insights sales can actually use, and sales acts on them within 2 weeks.

Most companies let these teams work in silos and wonder why buyers ghost them.

2️⃣ Intelligence
60% of buying research happens anonymously.

Snowflake doesn't wait for buyers to raise their hand. They track who's researching them before prospects even know Snowflake exists.

How? Intent data:
↳First-party signals (website visits, email clicks, product usage)
↳Third-party signals (research activity, job changes, funding rounds)

They use tools like Bombora to spot accounts showing interest in the background.

Accounts flagged as "high propensity" convert 4x better than average CRM accounts.

3️⃣ Activation

When an account shows buying signals, they launch their "1-2 Punch" play:
↳Weeks 1-2: ABM team warms the account with personalized campaigns (ads, emails, microsites tailored to that account's pain points) 

↳ Week 3: SDRs step in with outbound
By the time an SDR makes a call, the prospect already knows about Snowflake.
Result: 36/100 meeting rate on pre-warmed accounts (vs 10/100 on cold).

4️⃣ Engagement
They hit prospects from every angle:

↳Personalized email sequences
↳LinkedIn outreach
↳Calls
↳Custom microsites

Every touchpoint delivers the same message from Marketing → SDR → Sales.
The experience feels cohesive instead of disjointed.

Snowflake's personalized pages have a 4x higher meeting rate than non-personalized ones.

5️⃣ Optimization
They constantly analyze what's working.

Engagement metrics get shared between ABM, SDRs, and AEs in bi-weekly alignment meetings.

They adjust messaging and targeting on the fly.
Here's the kicker: They DON'T track attribution.
No fighting over who gets "credit" for the deal.

Instead, they measure impact at the account level and optimize the full funnel together.

The full results:
↳2-4x more meetings on ABM accounts
↳Deals close 2x faster from SQO to Won
↳Top-tier accounts convert 5x better
↳40% of Tier A accounts had open opportunities (vs 10% for lower tiers)

You don't need Snowflake's budget or 300 SDRs to use this.

Start here: 
↳ Align Marketing, SDRs, Sales on shared account goals 
↳ Track first-party intent (website visits, email engagement) 
↳ Launch ABM campaigns 2 weeks before SDR outreach 
↳ Measure account-level impact

P.S: Is your marketing team pre-warming accounts before SDRs reach out? Or are you still making cold calls?

time_posted: I grew my LinkedIn by 9,000+ followers in 2025.
hook: But honestly? That's not even the biggest thing that happened.
full_content:
But honestly? That's not even the biggest thing that happened.

12 months ago, I was working in fashion.

Today, I help run outbound for 8-9 figure B2B clients at a $7M ARR agency.

Here's what 2025 taught me:

1. Bet on yourself, even when it feels crazy

I made the jump from fashion to GTM tech with zero experience in tech.

Looking back, that discomfort and curiosity, drove me to work and learn as much as I could. Now I am more grateful than ever for that decision.

2. Learn from people who are where you want to be

I spent so much time studying Monika Grycz 💌's content , Alex Vacca 🧠🛠️'s business skills, and Michel Lieben 🧠's story of ColdIQ.

 My first month literally felt like I was in a classroom everyday.

Watch what's working for them. Break it down. Then make my own interpretation. 

3. Reps add up faster than you think

At ColdIQ, I went from working on 2-3 client accounts to now leading strategy across 10+.

I've built multiple internal tech products for the team.

Helped generate millions in pipeline for our clients.

The me from 12 months ago wouldn't even recognize this version.

4. Meet your team in person

I visited Europe for the first time this year. Best trip of my life BTW. 

Finally got to work alongside Harry Mason, someone I genuinely consider one of my best friends now.

Slack just doesn't hit the same as actually being in the room together.

5. The right people change everything

I got to work with some of the sharpest minds in GTM, along the way.

Every single week I learned something that would've taken me forever to figure out on my own.

2026 is about going even better.

More clients. More systems. More content.

And a lot more time with the team IRL.

P.S - what's your New Year's resolution?

time_posted: As I make my way back to LinkedIn, figured I'd start off by highlighting some best intent signals for outbound and how we track them at ColdIQ.
hook: Across 100 outreach sequences, these 4 intent signals drove my best campaigns.
full_content:
Across 100 outreach sequences, these 4 intent signals drove my best campaigns.

Here’s exactly how we use each one:

1️⃣ Website Visitor Identification

→ Someone lands on your site
→ Visitor ID platform picks up the trail
→ You map: what page they hit + how you can help + what’s up at their company

We use:
Common Room
Instantly
RB2B
Unify
Warmly
Koala
MeetVisitor

(Works best if you get a steady flow of site traffic-otherwise, too noisy.)

2️⃣ Job Changes [2 ways]

A. Old user, new company
→ Reach out again. They already know you.
B. New decision maker in the seat (<3 months)
→ Send a congrats, then ask about what’s top of mind in their new role

We track these with:
Common Room
Unify
LoneScale
Clay

3️⃣ Job Openings

Most get this wrong with the “I saw you’re hiring SDRs, want an agency?” move.

Instead:

→ Read the job description
→ Look at what the new hire will be doing
→ Show how your solution makes their job easier-not replaces them

4️⃣ News Announcements

M&As, launches, funding rounds, leadership changes-each is a window when companies are open to help.

Examples:

→ M&As = need for training, fresh systems, new leaders
→ Product launches = more engineers, bigger marketing push

We track these with:
Common Room
Clay

BONUS: Not every signal works for every business.
Common Room’s “Signal Spectrum Map” plots buying intent against signal volume.
Find the 2-3 signals that fit your world best.

What intent signals have you seen the best results with?

time_posted: AI Agents are replacing employees.
hook: Some companies even trust them with corporate credit cards.
full_content:
Some companies even trust them with corporate credit cards.

We're seeing this disruption across multiple departments.

And by 2027, it's predicted that ~80% will adopt them.

That said, in many cases, what we believe is an AI agent... is just a clever workflow.

Let's look at a typical work task to illustrate this, via 3 scenarios.

For example:

→ Scheduling a quarterly business review with a major client.


A. Non-Agentic Workflows

Humans handle all the work, and leverage tools that act based on their instructions:

1. A manager remembers it's time for the business review.

2. They check their calendar and their CRM.

3. They ask ChatGPT to "draft an email to schedule a client call"

4. They complete the draft and send the email.

Human did all the thinking.
AI didn't take any initiative.
It just helped with content.


B. Agentic Workflow

AI is part of a system with automated triggers & logic.

1. A calendar automation gets triggered as it's been 90 days since the latest business review.

2. The automation checks for availability in the calendar of the manager.

3. The automation then triggers the drafting of an email to schedule a meeting.

4. The email is automatically sent.

The workflow is semi-autonomous.
It saves time... 

But won't adapt to unexpected scenarios (i.e: follow-up, rescheduling)


C. AI Agents

The Agent has a 'goal-oriented objective'.

In this case: "Keep client happy & engaged".

1. Agent constantly checks CRM, activity & past clients' conversations.

2. It detects it's time for the quarterly business review. It also checks billing tier & checks recent usage drop.

3. It drafts a personalized note ('Hey Maïa, I see your team recently added 4 users, but they don't seem to use the platform that much...") and suggests times to meet.

4. It sends the email and monitors replies. It'll automatically follow up to suggest more time slots if it doesn't get an answer within 2-5 days.

5; Once the meeting is set, it comes up with an agenda based on previous convos, and warns the CS team of potential red flags (i.e: low usage → high churn risk)

It coordinates multiple tools.
It's contextual & personalised.

It focuses on a 'bigger goal' (i.e, client satisfaction) instead of a 'small task' (i.e, drafting & sending an email).

P.S.: Are you automating your repetitive work with AI at the moment?

→Lines 2-3: How you solve it

time_posted: The exact Cold Email Playbook we've deployed for 200+ B2B ColdIQ clients.
hook: Generating millions in revenue across the board.
full_content:
Generating millions in revenue across the board.

I run this for each one of my clients, of course tailored to their ICP, business model, and offer.

Let's start:

1️⃣ MULTI-SIGNAL RESEARCH

I recently started stacking more than one trigger. That means:3

- Bad Glassdoor reviews
- Recent layoffs or weird churn spikes
- Hiring sprees
- Funding rounds, product launches, office moves
- Competitor mentions, big market moves, industry pivots
-Competitor launches
-Public data filings, on their companies goals or issues
-Industry outages, lawsuits

What's better than one trigger? Layering 2-3 of these to know *why* my email should even land in their inbox. Every prospect gets a real reason for the outreach, not another.

2️⃣ OPENING THEY CAN’T IGNORE

This is by far your most important line, honestly sometimes more important than the subject line.

- Ask before pitch: “
Saw you just promoted Sarah to Head of Sales. Is she handling the outbound motion herself or are you bringing in external help for the ramp?”
- Reference *something* they did: You mentioned you're doing $40K MRR with zero paid ads - that's exactly the profile we're seeing crush it with signal-based outbound. Are you layering any buying intent data yet or still running pure ICP?

If my opener could pass for a mass send, I delete it and start over(in most cases). Sometimes a pain point is so common multiple people in your sector have it.

3️⃣ VALUE FIRST

I stopped asking for time on people's calendars a long time ago. You really want to give this person something that is just independent value to them.

I've always been a strong believer and giving before asking, and cold email is the best place for this.

- Drop a case study: “We helped [company] 4x replies in fintech-curious if you want the breakdown?”
- Offer a resource: “Saw you’re hiring SDRs-here’s our top onboarding doc, used across X and Y.
- Give them something they can implement without you. "3 outbound ideas you should try before Q4"

Taking the manual time to really make these strong lead magnets will save you thousands of wasted sends. The point is to GIVE before asking.

4️⃣ CONVERSION THAT FEELS HUMAN

I lean into soft CTAs. No “can you meet Thursday at 2?” Instead:

- “Worth exploring?”
- “Is this even on your radar?”
- “Or should I talk to someone else on your team?”

To be honest no one says yes to a deal on email #1. Open a conversation, not a deal.

time_posted: I cut an outbound list from 10,000 contacts to 3,200. After changing one thing the replies jumped from 4.2% to 6.8%. Here’s what changed.

full_content:
The same campaign was run twice.

Same ICP. Same message. Same sequences.

First pass:
→ 10,000 contacts
→ 4.2% reply rate

Second pass:
→ 3,200 contacts
→ 6.8% reply rate

The only change was timing.

Firmographics alone stopped working for outbound in 2025.

VP of Sales at Series A SaaS, 50-200 employees looks perfect on paper.
But many of them:
→ Just signed 2-year contracts
→ Don't have room in their budget
→ Or even plan to leave in a few weeks

Perfect ICP, but just bad timing. Not everyone is always in need of your product. 

We fixed that by layering intent signals with Ρrοѕреο V2.

Client sells pipeline management software to SaaS.

Old targeting:
→ B2B SaaS, 50-200 employees, Series A-B, US-based
→ VP of Sales or CRO

Result: 10,000 contacts, 4.2% replies.

New targeting with Prospeo V2:
→ Same firmographics
→ Sales dept grew 30%+ last 90 days
→ Raised funding in last 6 months
→ Using Salesforce or HubSpot

Result: 3,200 contacts, 6.8% replies.

The reply quality changed too.

Old list replies:
→ “Not a priority right now.”
→ “Try again in 6 months.”

New list replies:
→ “This is something that is actually quite timely”
→ “What does your calendar look like?”

People already felt the pain when we reached out.

Here is the exact search workflow we use:

1) Start with firmographics
Industry, company size, funding stage, location.

2) Add growth signals
→ Department headcount growth
→ Recent funding rounds
→ Active job postings

3) Add tech stack filters
→ Current tools
→ Integrations we support
→ Competitors we replace

4) Filter for contact quality
→ Verified email
→ Verified mobile
→ No incomplete data

Export to CSV → enrich in Clay → push to Instantly.ai.

That is how we avoid burning through 50,000 contacts each quarter and instead hit 15,000 with 2x performance.

The three intent signals that win most:

1) Department growth
Sales team grew 30%+ in 90 days = scaling = needs infrastructure.

2) Recent funding
Fresh round = unfrozen budgets and leadership under pressure to act.

3) Job postings
Hiring SDRs = thinking about outbound.
Hiring sales ops = thinking about tools.
Active hiring = clear priority on that function.

Stack all three and you show up when the company is ready to buy, not when your sequence is ready to send.

time_posted: Most teams spend $50k on SDRs before spending $5000 on demand.
hook: Here's why that's backwards
full_content:
Here's why that's backwards


I see this all the time-teams go straight to cold outreach and paid ads, then ask why conversion rates are trash and CAC keeps climbing.

Here’s the real problem: You’re trying to capture demand that doesn’t exist yet.

After working with 200+ B2B companies, one pattern repeats over and over. Outbound flops if you skip the basics.

Here’s the right order:

→ Layer 1: FOUNDATION

Get clear on WHO you serve and WHY they should care.
• Define your ICP and personas
• Nail your value prop
• Study competitors
• Prove product-market fit

→ Layer 2: DEMAND CREATION

Now, make people actually want what you sell.
• Create helpful content (not fluff)
• Build SEO presence
• Share real case studies
• Host webinars
• Partner up

→ Layer 3: DEMAND CAPTURE

Now you chase prospects.
• Launch paid ads
• Start cold outbound
• Retarget website visitors
• Scale sales development

Most skip straight to Layer 3. Feels like “real sales work,” right? But it’s like fishing in an empty pond.

Build each layer fully before you move on.

I’ve seen outbound convert 3x better when prospects already know your name.

time_posted: How I'd build the perfect GTM strategy if we had to start from 0:
hook: (It's what we've done to hit $6M+ ARR)
full_content:
(It's what we've done to hit $6M+ ARR)

Let's break it down 👇

1/ I'd nail my positioning before touching any marketing channel

This is where most people mess up from day one. They think they know what problem they solve, but they're solving the wrong problem.

For example:

a. Study your top competitors obsessively
b. Map out the real pain prospects feel
c. Create an offer that sells itself
d. Price on value, not time spent

2/ Analyze your competitors

→ Study your top competitors obsessively
→ Take what works, then improve it (don't reinvent the wheel if you don't have to)
→ See how they package their offers, what proof they use, and where they fall short

Most people either ignore their competition completely or get intimidated by them. Both are wrong.

3/ Create an offer that sells itself

a. Who is this for?
b. What pain does it fix?
c. What outcome do they get?

Lay out your steps to result and what makes you different → Price on value, not time spent (trust me, this matters more than you think).

If you can't explain your offer in 30 seconds to a 12-year-old, it's too complicated.

4/ Build momentum with warm outreach + organic

Everyone wants to jump straight to cold outbound, but that's the completely wrong way to go about it.

a. Post your offer on LinkedIn, share your point of view
b. Focus on getting feedback and traction, not on making it perfect
c. Start with your network, old clients, warm leads (people who already know you)

I see people spending months perfecting their offer. Wrong move. Get it out there, get feedback, iterate.

4/ Layer on scalable lead gen

This is where the fun stuff comes in.

→ Use Clay, Instantly, or Apollo to run cold campaigns
→ Mix in AI for list building and writing custom copy
→ Wait for replies, positive and negative

But, don't do this until you've got steps 1-4 working. I see way too many people trying to scale systems that are completely broken from the start.

6️⃣ Refine the engine, then scale

This is what separates the agencies that hit $5M from the ones stuck at $500K.

→ Double down on what's working (cold, content, referrals, pick your winners)
→ You don't always need more tools, you need systems that will work for you
→ Build simple systems for repeat work

Most people keep adding complexity when they should be adding systems.

Which of these steps do you find the hardest to nail? Or which one are you going to try next?

♻️ Follow me Kenny for the sales tools that built our $400k MRR agency.

After deploying 30+ AI Agents, here are the 3 architectures every scaling GTM team should know.


Here's what actually works:

True agentic systems don’t break the moment things get weird. They adapt, learn, and drive revenue without hand-holding.

Here are the 3 frameworks you should know before you build you next Ai agent.

→ 1️⃣ ReAct Agent (Reasoning + Acting)

ReAct agents run a loop:
Think → Research → Execute → Observe → Adjust

Real-world moves:
• Analyzes SEC filings for budget signals
• Scans job posts for new growth roles
• Checks competitor win/loss patterns
• Reviews leadership changes
• Maps tech stack for integration plays



→ 2️⃣ CodeAct Agent (Code + Action)

Writes custom code on demand. Solves problems in minutes, not hours.

Real time use case: Dynamic Territory Optimization Agent:
• Pulls live data from Salesforce, HubSpot, and more
• Runs 1000+ territory scenarios in minutes
• Factors in rep strengths, customer needs, and market shifts
• Builds real-time dashboards
• Adjusts territories as new accounts show up


→ 3️⃣ Agentic RAG (Retrieval + Generation)

Not just searching. Hunting, evaluating, synthesizing from 50+ sources.

Real time use case: Competitive Intelligence Agent:
• Monitors competitor sites, G2, job boards, social posts, patent filings
• Tracks pricing and proposal changes
• Flags competitor churn by watching LinkedIn job moves
• Auto-generates battle cards and objection scripts
• Builds ROI calculators on the fly

These aren’t automations. They’re essentially teammates that execute needed task, that are time consuming.

time_posted: I just spent 9 hours dissecting Google's official AI prompt engineering course. The results will change how you think about using AI in business.
hook: The 5-Step Business AI Framework 👇
full_content:
The 5-Step Business AI Framework 👇

1️⃣ TASK → Be surgical, not general
❌ "Write a marketing plan"
✅ "Create a 90-day customer acquisition strategy for B2B SaaS companies with 50-500 employees"

2️⃣ CONTEXT → Feed the machine your reality
→ Industry landscape
→ Competitive positioning
→ Current challenges
→ Target audience specifics
→ Business goals, timelines

3️⃣ REFERENCES → Show, don’t just tell
→ Past campaigns
→ Competitor examples
→ Brand voice samples
→ Customer feedback

4️⃣ EVALUATE → Quality control everything
→ Would this drive the outcome I need?
→ Does this sound like my brand?
→ Would my target audience respond?
→ Is this actionable or just fluff?

5️⃣ ITERATE → Perfection through repetition
Most people stop at the first output. Winners refine 3-5 times.

Real business examples:

For Sales Leaders:
Instead of "Write a cold email sequence"
Try: "Act as a senior sales consultant. Create a 5-touch cold email sequence for CFOs at mid-market manufacturing companies struggling with cash flow visibility. Address Q1 budget planning. Position our dashboard as the solution. Use a consultative tone and include industry stats. Reference top performer emails [attach]."

For Marketing Leaders:
Instead of "Create social media content"
Try: "You're a content strategist for B2B tech companies. Develop 10 LinkedIn post ideas that position our CEO as a supply chain thought leader. Educate supply chain directors at Fortune 1000s, drive engagement, and show our expertise. Use our brand voice [attach] and reference high-performing industry posts."

For Product Leaders:
Instead of "Help with product positioning"
Try: "Act as a product marketing expert. Analyze our feature set and create positioning statements for three buyer personas: technical users, business users, economic buyers. Differentiate us from [Competitor A/B], address pain points, and connect to business outcomes. Use jobs-to-be-done. Reference successful examples [attach]."

Advanced iteration hacks:

→ Add more context layers (trends, seasonality, constraints)
→ Break complex requests into steps (Research → Framework → Strategy → Tactics → Metrics)
→ Switch perspectives (problem vs solution focus, customer vs internal)
→ Use smart constraints ("Max 100 words", "No jargon", "Include metrics", "End with CTA")

If you want to actually remember all this, I built a prompt engineering cheat sheet below.

full_content:
Here’s a direction of Ramp’s outbound strategy.


Their reps book 3-4x the meetings of anyone else in fintech.

Here's what I found: They don't rely on brute force. Instead, they built a testing machine that runs a plethora of experiments every 90 days, paired with ML systems that identify 75% of their best opportunities before anyone picks up the phone.

The output: 40,000+ qualified sales opportunities.

Here's what makes them different:

1️⃣ Speed Over Perfection While enterprise companies usually debate concepts for months, Ramp executes in days.
Their approach: 
↳ Run north of 100+ tests quarterly, all logged in Airtable 
↳ Weekly KPI meetings: What worked stays, everything else goes away 
↳ Track pipeline payback instead of traditional metrics (gives you faster signals)

2️⃣ Engineers For Sales Usually companies silo engineering away from GTM. Ramp does the opposite. They have developers building tools specifically for their outbound teams.

How their growth team operates:
↳ Dedicated engineers automate repetitive sales tasks
 ↳ Test ideas in rapid 2-week cycles 
↳ Invest heavily in data quality 
↳ Success = pipeline created

3️⃣ Experimentation Squads 
Setup: 

↳ team of Growth lead, SDR, Data person, Engineer 
↳ 30-day maximum timeline with isolated tracking

4️⃣ Segmented Outreach Channels

High-Value Accounts: 
↳ Reps manually craft every message 
↳ Delivered through personal Gmail (maximizes deliverability)

Medium Accounts: 
↳ AI drafts personalized copy 
↳ Human reviews before sending from Gmail 
↳ Gets 44% more responses than sequence tools

Long-Tail Accounts: 
↳ Volume plays through automation platforms 
↳ Same core message, optimized for scale


5️⃣ Strategic AI Integration Ramp treats AI like a work multiplier, not necessarily replacement.

Their team uses it to eliminate busywork so reps focus on relationships.

Their AI finds personalization hooks like connecting a prospect's alma mater to upcoming games, then uses that to start conversations.

6️⃣ Infrastructure Built For Speed Ramp's tech stack is designed around one thing: move fast without breaking. What they run:
↳ Snowflake powers instant access to all GTM data (no lag, no crashes) ↳ Hightouch pushes warehouse data directly into CRM within minutes ↳ Waterfall.io orchestrates 5 data vendors to maximize contact coverage ↳ Custom ML model routes accounts to the right channel automatically (email, call, direct mail, ads)

TLDR 
↳ Replace monthly planning with weekly experiments (force the discipline) 
↳ Hire 1 technical person to automate list building and data flows 
↳ Pay your SDRs for pipeline quality, not meeting quantity 
↳ Build a second outbound track on separate infrastructure for clean testing 
↳ Deploy AI for scoring and admin work, keep humans in the relationship 
↳ Create a public win-sharing channel so your best tactics spread instantly

P.S: What's the ONE thing from Ramp's playbook you'd implement first?

time_posted: After working with 70+ clients and analyzing 1,000+ positive LinkedIn replies, here's what I learned (and a full SOP breakdown).
hook: Here's what I discovered:
full_content:
Here's what I discovered:

The 6 insights that changed everything:

→ 1. The hidden 2-inbox problem Most people don't know LinkedIn gives you two separate inboxes:

Sales Navigator inbox (where you prospect)
Native LinkedIn inbox (where replies actually land)
The mistake: Messaging from Sales Nav sends to "Other" folder The fix: Always switch to native inbox for messaging

→ 2. The star system that saves deals Elite performers use LinkedIn's features differently:

★ = Hot prospects who showed interest (max 5 total) Unread = Waiting on their reply Archive = Done/not ready

The rule: Always handle starred conversations first Why it works: You never ignore buying signals for cold outreach

→ 3. The 3-touch cadence that converts We tested 47 different follow-up sequences across our clients.

The winner: Day 1: Initial personalized message 
Day 3: Different format follow-up (voice if first was text)
Day 6: Value-add final touch with resource/insight

Key insight: Hit different weekdays to catch varying schedules

→ 4. Voice notes = cheat code Analyzed 2,847 LinkedIn messages across our client base:

Text messages get buried in noise Voice messages demand attention Video messages build instant rapport.

The formula that works:

25-30 seconds maximum
Reference something specific from their profile
Enthusiastic but professional tone
Clear call-to-action at the end

Pro tip: Rotate formats based on prospect's communication style

→ 5. The time-boxing method Successful clients check LinkedIn DMs only 3x daily:

9am: Handle starred (hot) prospects first
1pm: Process new messages + send outreach
4pm: Follow-up on pending conversations

Why this works:
Eliminates constant context-switching
Makes replies more intentional
Protects focus for other revenue activities

→ 6. The message templates that convert Stop rewriting the same messages. 

Here are our highest-performing snippets:

Pain follow-up: "Typically when I speak with [persona], they say [specific pain point] is their biggest challenge. Sound familiar?"

Educational value-add: "Sharing this [specific resource] - thought you'd find the part about [relevant insight] interesting given [their situation]"

Referral transition: "Would it make more sense for me to connect with [specific colleague] about this?"

Here's the full notion breakdown - https://lnkd.in/gWykTVx9
